pattern: Since a special path only breaks when a value repeats somewhere on the way down from an ancestor, exploring downward from every possible starting ancestor while tracking which values have already appeared on that particular walk reveals exactly where each candidate path is forced to stop.
hints: A path starting at a chosen ancestor and going downward stays valid only as long as no node's value has already appeared earlier on that same walk -- the instant a value repeats, that branch can go no further as a special path.

Explore downward from every node as a potential top ancestor, keeping a set of values seen so far on the current walk; whenever the current node's value already appears in that set, stop exploring beneath it entirely, since every deeper node would still carry the same repeated value on its path back to this ancestor.
Track the best length found so far, and among all paths achieving that best length, keep the smallest node count -- a single node by itself is always a valid special path of length zero and one node.

edge_inputs.description: small star-shaped tree with a repeated value on one branch
a simple chain of four distinct values
smallest possible tree, a single edge
smallest possible tree with distinct values
every value distinct so the whole tree is one giant special path
all values identical, every special path is a single node
a repeat several levels down forces two separate ancestors to both reach the same length, testing the tie-break on node count

stress.description: random trees with random edge lengths and deliberately repeated values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 300))
    edges = []
    for i in range(1, n):
        parent = rng.randint(0, i - 1)
        length = rng.randint(1, 1000)
        edges.append([parent, i, length])
    pool = [rng.randint(0, 9) for _ in range(max(1, n // 4))]
    nums = [rng.choice(pool) for _ in range(n)]
    return [edges, nums]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def longestSpecialPath(self, edges, nums):
        n = len(nums)
        adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]
        for u, v, w in edges:
            adj[u].append((v, w))
            adj[v].append((u, w))
        parent = [-1] * n
        parent_w = [0] * n
        depth_len = [0] * n
        order = [0]
        seen_bfs = [False] * n
        seen_bfs[0] = True
        head = 0
        while head < len(order):
            node = order[head]
            head += 1
            for nxt, w in adj[node]:
                if not seen_bfs[nxt]:
                    seen_bfs[nxt] = True
                    parent[nxt] = node
                    parent_w[nxt] = w
                    depth_len[nxt] = depth_len[node] + w
                    order.append(nxt)
        best_len = -1
        best_nodes = 0
        for d in order:
            chain = []
            cur = d
            while cur != -1:
                chain.append(cur)
                cur = parent[cur]
            chain.reverse()
            for start_idx in range(len(chain)):
                sub = chain[start_idx:]
                vals = [nums[node] for node in sub]
                if len(vals) != len(set(vals)):
                    continue
                a = sub[0]
                length = depth_len[d] - depth_len[a]
                node_count = len(sub)
                if length > best_len or (length == best_len and node_count < best_nodes):
                    best_len = length
                    best_nodes = node_count
        return [best_len, best_nodes]

complexity.time: O(n^2) worst case for the authored reference; a linear sliding-window-on-the-tree technique exists for the full constraint scale
